Turn them ON for NVT and NPT equilibration of any protein or protein-in-membrane system, and OFF for production. Never restrain during production unless the goal explicitly requires a fixed reference (e.g. a ligand-in-site sampling study).

label: Position restraint force constant
meaning: Harmonic spring constant tethering each restrained heavy atom to its reference position, per Cartesian direction.
cost_impact: none
agent_guidance: Standard is 1000 on all protein heavy atoms. For a strained or homology/AlphaFold model, ramp restraints down across successive NPT stages (1000 -> 500 -> 200 -> 100 -> 0) to avoid a structural shock when they are released; this is the fix for 'protein blows up / RMSD jumps at t=0 of production'.

label: Restrained group
meaning: Which atoms carry the position restraints during equilibration.
cost_impact: none
agent_guidance: Use protein_heavy for standard equilibration. Use protein_backbone or protein_ca when you deliberately want side chains to relax (e.g. after a mutation or a docking pose). For a bilayer with an embedded protein, restrain protein_heavy and let the lipids anneal around it.

label: Reference coordinate scaling
meaning: How the position-restraint reference coordinates are transformed when the barostat rescales the box; required for a physically consistent restrained-NPT stage.
cost_impact: none
agent_guidance: MANDATORY whenever -DPOSRES and pressure coupling are both on — grompp warns and the restraints become nonsense with refcoord-scaling=no. Use com for a solute restrained in a box; all is acceptable for restrained solvent/lipids.

label: Raw mdp overrides
meaning: Free-form dict of engine keys merged last into the stage's generated .mdp, providing an escape hatch for any of the 200+ GROMACS settings the ontology does not name.
cost_impact: depends on the key
agent_guidance: Prefer named ontology params — they are validated. Use overrides only for exotic keys (pull-*, annealing-*, disre*, freezegrps). Every override must be echoed into the manifest so the resolved physics is auditable; the current lab records params but never the resolved .mdp, which blocks self-correction.

label: mdrun wall-clock limit
meaning: Hard wall-clock cap on the stage; mdrun writes a checkpoint and exits cleanly at the limit instead of being killed and losing all work.
cost_impact: Caps wall time exactly; work up to the cap is preserved via the checkpoint.
agent_guidance: Set this on every production stage when the user gives a GPU budget. Combine with stage.type=extend to chunk a long run: run 2 h, checkpoint, resubmit with -cpi. This is how you honor 'stay under my GPU budget' without throwing away the trajectory — the current kill-based pause/resume loses everything.

label: Checkpoint interval
meaning: How often mdrun writes the binary checkpoint (.cpt) containing coordinates, velocities, box, and coupling state needed for a bitwise-continuable restart.
cost_impact: Negligible; a checkpoint write is a few MB of I/O.
agent_guidance: Lower to 5 min for jobs that may be preempted/paused by the scheduler. The checkpoint is what makes restart possible; without it a killed 10 ns run restarts from zero.

label: Restart / continue from checkpoint
meaning: Path to a previous stage's .cpt used to resume an interrupted stage (-cpi) or to seed the next stage's velocities and box (grompp -t).
cost_impact: Saves all previously computed steps.
agent_guidance: On resume after pause/kill, always pass -cpi with -append so the xtc/edr continue rather than being overwritten. When simply chaining stages, pass the .cpt to grompp -t (not just the .gro) — a .gro alone drops velocities and silently forces re-thermalization.

label: Extend an existing run
meaning: Lengthen an already-completed stage by editing its .tpr rather than restarting from scratch, so a run judged 'not converged' can be continued.
cost_impact: Adds exactly the requested time.
agent_guidance: This is the agent's primary self-correction lever: if RMSD has not plateaued, or the MSD fit window is too short, or block averages of energy disagree, extend rather than re-run. Cheapest possible way to fix an under-converged result.

label: Minimizer
meaning: Which energy-minimization algorithm relaxes the starting structure's steric clashes before dynamics.
cost_impact: steep is cheapest per step; cg/l-bfgs converge in fewer steps but cost more per step and cannot be used with constraints/domain decomposition in all cases.
agent_guidance: Use steep by default — it is robust against the huge forces in a freshly solvated box. If steep stalls above emtol, chain a short steep (few hundred steps) followed by cg to reach a tight emtol (e.g. for a QM-ready or normal-mode-ready structure). l-bfgs only for gas-phase/implicit systems (no domain decomposition).

label: Minimization force tolerance
meaning: Convergence criterion: minimization stops when the maximum force on any atom falls below this value, so it measures how relaxed the structure is.
cost_impact: Tighter tolerance costs more steps; going from 1000 to 10 can be 10-100x more EM steps, but EM is minutes at most.
agent_guidance: 1000 is fine before MD — the thermostat will handle the rest. Tighten to 100-250 if the first NVT stage crashes with 'LINCS warning' or 'water molecule cannot be settled', which means the start structure was still clashing. Tighten to <=10 only when the minimized structure itself is the deliverable (QM handoff, normal modes).

label: Minimization initial step size
meaning: Initial trial displacement of the steepest-descent minimizer; it is adapted upward on success and halved on failure.
cost_impact: Too large wastes steps on rejected moves; too small needs many more steps. Second-order effect only.
agent_guidance: Leave at 0.01. Reduce to 0.001-0.005 if EM immediately reports 'Steepest Descents converged to machine precision' with a still-huge Fmax (a sign the very first step exploded on an overlapping atom). Coarse-grained beads tolerate 0.02-0.05.

label: Max minimization steps
meaning: Upper bound on minimization iterations; the run stops at emtol or here, whichever comes first.
cost_impact: EM is cheap; even 50000 steps on a 50k-atom box is a few minutes on GPU.
agent_guidance: Rarely worth touching. If EM exits on nsteps rather than emtol, that is a diagnostic: the structure is bad (clashing ligand, missing hydrogens, overlapping ions) — fix the build, do not just raise nsteps.

label: Reported Fmax (observable)
meaning: The maximum force actually reached at the end of minimization, parsed from the EM log — the single best cheap predictor of whether the following dynamics will crash.
cost_impact: none
agent_guidance: Record in the manifest summary. If Fmax > ~5x emtol at exit, do not submit production — re-minimize or rebuild. This is a gate the agent should check between stages.

label: Compressed trajectory write interval
meaning: Number of integration steps between frames written to the compressed .xtc trajectory — this sets the time resolution of every structural analysis and the size of the trajectory file.
default: derived from xtc_interval_ps / dt (typically 5000 steps = 10 ps at dt=0.002)
cost_impact: Frame count = duration/interval. Each all-atom frame ≈ 3 bytes/atom at precision 1000; 50k atoms ≈ 150 kB/frame. Writing every step can cost 10-30% runtime in I/O and produce GB-scale files.
agent_guidance: Set via the time-domain alias, not steps. Rule of thumb: keep 1000-5000 frames total for the stage (interval_ps ≈ duration_ps/2000). RMSD/Rg/SASA/DSSP: 10-20 ps is plenty. RDF/density: 1-10 ps. H-bond lifetimes, MSD of water, PCA of fast modes: 0.5-2 ps. Never write every step for a 10 ns box unless you specifically need velocity/force autocorrelations.

label: Trajectory write interval (time)
meaning: Physical time between saved trajectory frames; the human/agent-facing form of nstxout-compressed.
cost_impact: Inversely proportional to trajectory size and analysis I/O time.
agent_guidance: Always express output in ps. Must satisfy interval_ps >= dt and ideally be an exact multiple of dt. If the requested analysis needs correlation times shorter than the interval (e.g. hbond lifetime, D from MSD), shrink it — a coarse interval silently makes those analyses garbage.

label: XTC coordinate precision
meaning: Reciprocal of the coordinate rounding used by the lossy xtc compressor: 1000 means coordinates are stored to 1/1000 nm = 0.001 nm = 0.01 A.
cost_impact: File size scales roughly with log(precision); 10000 costs ~30-40% more bytes than 1000, 100 saves ~25%.
agent_guidance: Leave at 1000 for all structural analysis (RMSD, RDF, SASA, H-bonds are insensitive to 0.001 nm noise). Raise to 10000 only if you will compute something sensitive to fine positions (e.g. numerically differentiating positions, or feeding coordinates back into a QM step). Lower to 100 only to shrink an archival membrane trajectory.

label: Energy write interval
meaning: Steps between writes of energies, temperature, pressure, box and density to the .edr file — this sets the sampling of every thermodynamic observable and of the equilibration diagnostics.
default: derived from energy_interval_ps / dt (typically 500 steps = 1 ps)
cost_impact: Very cheap: an .edr frame is ~100 bytes. Even 1 ps sampling of a 100 ns run is a few MB.
agent_guidance: Sample energy MUCH more often than coordinates — it is nearly free and it is what you use to decide whether the system is equilibrated (density plateau, T/P mean and drift, potential-energy drift). 0.5-2 ps is right. Must be a multiple of nstcalcenergy (default 100) or grompp will adjust it. The current derived rule (nstenergy = nstxout/2) undersamples energy badly for long runs.

label: Energy write interval (time)
meaning: Physical time between .edr energy frames; the agent-facing form of nstenergy.
cost_impact: negligible
agent_guidance: Keep at 1 ps. Drop to 0.1 ps if you need a pressure autocorrelation (e.g. viscosity) — pressure is extremely noisy and needs dense sampling.

label: Energy computation interval
meaning: How often global energies are actually computed (as opposed to written); it also gates the allowed values of nstenergy and forces a global communication step.
cost_impact: Setting it to 1 forces a global reduction every step and can cost 5-15% on GPU runs.
agent_guidance: Leave at 100. Only lower it if you need energies at a finer interval than 100 steps; if you set nstenergy < nstcalcenergy, grompp silently changes nstcalcenergy for you and you lose GPU performance.

label: Log write interval
meaning: Steps between human-readable energy/performance blocks in the .log file — the source of the performance number (ns/day) and of thermostat/barostat diagnostics.
default: derived from log_interval_ps / dt (typically 5000 steps = 10 ps)
cost_impact: negligible
agent_guidance: Set to roughly the trajectory interval (10 ps). Its real value to the agent is the log's ns/day and load-imbalance report, which is how you decide whether to change GPU offload flags or accept the cost estimate. Must be a multiple of nstcalcenergy.

label: Full-precision trajectory interval
meaning: Steps between frames written to the uncompressed .trr, which is the only file that can also carry velocities and forces.
cost_impact: HUGE: a .trr frame is ~12 bytes/atom for x alone, 24 with v, 36 with f — 10-30x an xtc frame. Leave at 0.
agent_guidance: Keep 0 in almost every case; the .xtc plus the final .gro/.cpt is enough. Turn it on only for: velocity autocorrelation / vibrational spectra (needs nstvout at 5-20 fs), force-matching or QM/MM handoff, or a normal-mode analysis. If you do turn it on, shorten the stage — this is a classic way to fill a disk.

label: Velocity write interval
meaning: Steps between velocity frames in the .trr; needed for any dynamical quantity computed from velocities rather than positions.
cost_impact: Doubles .trr size; dense settings (every 10 steps) generate GB/ns.
agent_guidance: Only for VACF-based transport coefficients or IR spectra. Otherwise 0 — the checkpoint already preserves velocities for restarts, which is the only other reason people think they need this.

label: Force write interval
meaning: Steps between force frames in the .trr.
cost_impact: Same order as nstvout; largest per-frame payload.
agent_guidance: Essentially only for force-matching / coarse-grained parameterization or QM/MM validation. Never enable for a normal production run.

label: Groups written to the trajectory
meaning: Restricts the .xtc to a subset of atoms (e.g. only the protein), which is the single biggest lever on trajectory size for a solvated system.
cost_impact: For a 50k-atom box with a 2k-atom protein, writing Protein only is ~25x smaller: a 10 ns run drops from ~200 MB to ~5-8 MB and analyses run 20x faster.
agent_guidance: Set to Protein (or Non-Water) whenever the requested analyses are solute-only (RMSD, Rg, RMSF, SASA, DSSP, PCA). Do NOT set it if any requested analysis needs solvent: RDF involving water, water MSD/diffusion, density profiles, protein-water H-bonds, or PBC re-imaging that requires whole-system context. Decide this from the analysis list before submitting — it cannot be recovered afterwards.

label: Energy groups
meaning: Splits the reported nonbonded energies into per-group and group-pair contributions (e.g. Protein-Water LJ and Coulomb) so interaction energies can be read from the .edr.
cost_impact: On GPU this can force parts of the nonbonded work back to the CPU and cost 20-50% — GROMACS does not support energy-group decomposition in the GPU nonbonded kernels for all cases.
agent_guidance: Avoid on GPU production runs. If the user wants a protein-ligand interaction energy, prefer a short CPU rerun (`gmx mdrun -rerun traj.xtc`) with energygrps set, over paying the cost for the whole production run. Note: these are NOT physical binding free energies — say so when reporting them.

label: COM motion removal interval
meaning: How often the center-of-mass drift of the system (or of comm-grps) is removed, preventing the 'flying ice cube' where the whole box acquires kinetic energy and internal degrees of freedom cool.
cost_impact: negligible
agent_guidance: Leave at 100 (GROMACS ties it to nstcalcenergy). Only revisit if the log shows persistent COM drift or the temperature reported for a group looks systematically low.

label: COM motion removal mode
meaning: Which kind of bulk motion is removed: linear translation, translation+rotation (for a non-periodic cluster), or none.
cost_impact: negligible
agent_guidance: Linear for any periodic box. Angular ONLY for a vacuum/implicit-solvent single molecule (pbc=no) — using Angular under PBC is wrong. Use Linear-acceleration-correction with sd/leap-frog + heavy COM removal if you see a slow energy drift.

label: Viewer frame cap
meaning: Maximum number of frames the engine down-samples the trajectory to when converting to the PDB the 3Dmol viewer loads; a presentation-only limit that must not affect analysis.
cost_impact: Browser memory only; ~1-3 MB per 100 frames of a small system.
agent_guidance: Never change this to get better statistics — analyses must run on the full .xtc, not the down-sampled viewer PDB. Raise only if the user complains the animation is choppy, and only for small systems.

label: Trajectory PBC/centering for viewing and analysis
meaning: How periodic images are undone before viewing or analysis: making molecules whole, keeping them in the box, and centering the solute — without this a protein appears split across the box edge and RMSD/Rg are nonsense.
cost_impact: One extra pass over the trajectory; seconds to minutes.
agent_guidance: Use a two-step canonical pipeline before analysis: (1) -pbc whole (or mol) -center -ur compact for structural analyses (RMSD, Rg, RMSF, SASA, DSSP, PCA); (2) -pbc nojump for anything involving displacement over time (MSD/diffusion) — using -pbc mol for MSD produces a catastrophically wrong D because molecules teleport across the box. RDF and density should be run on the raw trajectory with the analysis tool's own PBC handling, not on a re-imaged file.

label: Analysis start time (discard equilibration)
meaning: Trajectory time before which frames are discarded from an analysis, so that residual equilibration does not bias the average.
default: 10% of production duration, or the observed density/RMSD plateau time
cost_impact: Fewer frames analyzed; slightly faster.
agent_guidance: ALWAYS set this. Choose it by looking at the observable itself: for density/box use the .edr plateau; for a protein use the point where RMSD stops rising. Reporting an average over the first, un-relaxed nanosecond is the most common way to get a wrong number out of a technically successful run.

label: Analysis end time
meaning: Trajectory time after which frames are ignored, used to analyze a sub-window (e.g. compare first vs second half as a convergence test).
cost_impact: negligible
agent_guidance: Use to implement the block/half-split convergence check: compute the observable on [t/2, 3t/4] and [3t/4, t]; if they disagree beyond their error bars, the run is not converged — extend it rather than reporting the number.

label: Analysis frame stride
meaning: Only read every Nth frame of the trajectory during analysis, trading statistics for speed.
cost_impact: Linear speedup of the analysis pass.
agent_guidance: Leave at the native interval — analysis is cheap compared to the MD. Only stride for very large membrane trajectories, and never for correlation-based analyses (MSD, H-bond autocorrelation), where striding destroys the short-time behaviour you are fitting.

label: Error estimation blocks
meaning: Number of contiguous blocks the production window is split into for block averaging, giving a defensible error bar on any time-averaged observable.
cost_impact: none
agent_guidance: Every scalar an agent reports (density, APL, mean Rg, D, mean RMSD) MUST come with a block-averaged uncertainty. A number without an error bar cannot support a claim like 'it stays folded'. Use >=5 blocks each longer than the observable's correlation time.

label: Radial distribution function
meaning: Probability of finding an atom of the selection group at distance r from the reference group, normalized by the ideal-gas density — the fingerprint of liquid structure and solvation shells.
default: {ref: 'OW', sel: 'OW', bin: 0.002, rmax: min(1.5, 0.5*box), begin_ps: analysis.begin_ps, norm: 'rdf', selrpos: 'atom' }
options: ref/sel = atom names, residue names, or index groups
depends_on: box size (rmax <= half the shortest box vector)
cost_impact: Seconds to a few minutes; scales with frames x pairs.
agent_guidance: rmax must not exceed half the smallest box vector — beyond that the RDF is contaminated by the minimum image and flattens artificially. Use bin=0.002 nm for water (to resolve the 0.28 nm first peak) and 0.01-0.02 nm for coarse-grained beads. For water use OW-OW; for ion solvation use NA-OW and read the first-shell coordination number by integrating 4*pi*rho*r^2*g(r) to the first minimum. Needs only 100-500 well-spaced frames to converge — do not run 10 ns just for an RDF.

label: Mean-square displacement / diffusion coefficient
meaning: Average squared displacement of a selection versus lag time; its long-time slope gives the self-diffusion coefficient via the Einstein relation D = slope/(2*d).
unit: nm^2 vs ps; D in 1e-5 cm^2/s
default: {sel: 'Water', type: '3D', beginfit_frac: 0.2, endfit_frac: 0.8, mol: false, pbc: 'nojump'}
cost_impact: Seconds; but demands a long, densely sampled trajectory to be meaningful.
agent_guidance: Three ways this goes wrong and the agent must guard all three: (1) trajectory not unwrapped -> use -pbc nojump; (2) fit window includes the ballistic/sub-diffusive short-time regime -> fit only the linear middle (roughly 20-80% of the max lag) and check the log-log slope is ~1; (3) too short a run -> D from <1 ns of lag is unreliable. For a membrane use type=lateral_xy with mol=true (lipid COM). Always correct/caveat the strong system-size (finite-size, Yeh-Hummer) dependence of D when reporting.

label: RMSD vs reference
meaning: Root-mean-square deviation of the selection from a reference structure after least-squares fitting — the primary measure of whether a protein has stayed folded or drifted from its crystal structure.
default: {fit_group: 'Backbone', calc_group: 'Backbone', reference: 'em.gro or start.pdb', tu: 'ns', pbc: 'whole'}
options: fit_group / calc_group: Backbone | C-alpha | Protein | Protein-H | MainChain
cost_impact: Seconds.
agent_guidance: This is the headline observable for the lysozyme-stability class of goals. Fit on Backbone and compute on Backbone (or C-alpha). Interpretation for a small globular protein at 300-310 K: plateau below ~0.2-0.25 nm = folded and stable; a monotonic rise that has not plateaued by the end = not converged, EXTEND; a step change to >0.4-0.5 nm = partial unfolding or a domain motion — inspect RMSF and DSSP before claiming unfolding. Always reference against the MINIMIZED structure and always discard equilibration. Never report a single mean RMSD without the time series and a plateau check.

label: Radius of gyration
meaning: Mass-weighted RMS distance of the selection's atoms from its center of mass — a compactness measure that detects global expansion (unfolding) or collapse.
options: components: also report Rg about x/y/z principal axes
cost_impact: Seconds.
agent_guidance: Use as the independent cross-check on RMSD: a folded protein has a flat Rg (lysozyme ≈ 1.4-1.45 nm). RMSD up + Rg flat = a rigid-body/domain motion or a poor fit group, NOT unfolding. RMSD up + Rg up = genuine expansion. Report the pair together; the agent should never claim unfolding on RMSD alone. Enable components to detect anisotropic elongation.

label: Per-residue RMSF (fluctuations)
meaning: Root-mean-square fluctuation of each atom/residue about its average position over the analysis window — a per-residue map of flexibility, comparable to crystallographic B-factors.
default: {group: 'C-alpha', res: true, fit: true, begin_ps: analysis.begin_ps, output_bfac: true}
options: fit: least-squares fit to the reference first
output_bfac: write B-factors into a PDB
cost_impact: Seconds.
agent_guidance: Run on the EQUILIBRATED window only — including equilibration inflates every residue's RMSF. Typical: 0.05-0.1 nm in secondary-structure cores, 0.2-0.4+ nm in loops and termini. Use it to localize what the RMSD is seeing: if the RMSD rise is entirely from the terminal residues, the fold is intact — say that explicitly. Compare to experimental B-factors via B = (8*pi^2/3)*RMSF^2 when a crystal structure is available.

label: Density (bulk and profile)
meaning: Either the scalar mass density of the box or a one-dimensional profile of density along an axis, sliced by group — the standard validation of a solvent box and the primary structural readout of a bilayer.
options: groups: System | Water | POPC | Headgroups(P) | Tails | Protein
depends_on: ensemble must be NPT for a meaningful bulk density
compressed-x-grps must include all groups profiled
cost_impact: Seconds.
agent_guidance: Bulk mode is the #1 equilibration gate: TIP3P/SPC-E water at 300 K and 1 bar must land at ~995-1000 kg/m^3. If your NPT density is off by more than ~1%, the force field/cutoff/DispCorr settings are wrong — stop and fix them before spending GPU on production. Profile mode (axis=z, center on the bilayer, groups = Water/Headgroups/Tails) gives the bilayer thickness (P-P peak separation) and is how you verify a membrane is intact and not interdigitated.

label: Hydrogen bonds
meaning: Counts and lifetimes of hydrogen bonds between two groups using a donor-acceptor distance and a hydrogen-donor-acceptor angle criterion.
unit: count vs ns; lifetime in ps
options: group pairs: Protein-Protein | Protein-Water | Water-Water | Protein-Ligand
depends_on: compressed-x-grps must contain BOTH groups (water H-bonds need water in the xtc)
cost_impact: Minutes on a large solvated system; the pair search is the expensive part.
agent_guidance: Use the conventional geometric criteria (0.35 nm, 30 deg) unless the user asks otherwise — changing them changes the count and makes the number incomparable to the literature. Intra-protein H-bond count is a sensitive, cheap unfolding detector: a persistent drop tracks secondary-structure loss and corroborates DSSP. For LIFETIMES you must have saved frames at <=1-2 ps — if the trajectory interval is 10 ps, refuse to report a lifetime and rerun with finer output instead of producing a meaningless number.

label: Solvent-accessible surface area
meaning: Area of the solute surface reachable by the center of a spherical solvent probe, optionally split into hydrophobic and hydrophilic contributions and reported per residue.
options: output: total | hydrophobic | hydrophilic | per_residue | per_atom | volume
cost_impact: Minutes for a large protein at high ndots; scales with frames x atoms x ndots.
agent_guidance: Third leg of the folded/unfolded triad (with RMSD and Rg): unfolding exposes buried hydrophobic surface, so a rising HYDROPHOBIC SASA is a much more specific unfolding signal than total SASA. Keep probe_radius at 0.14 nm (water) for comparability. Raise ndots to 100-200 only if the curve is visibly noisy; 24 is fine for time series.

label: Secondary structure (DSSP)
meaning: Per-residue, per-frame assignment of secondary structure (helix, sheet, turn, coil) from backbone H-bond geometry — the most direct, interpretable evidence of whether a fold is retained.
depends_on: compressed-x-grps must include the full protein backbone incl. H or allow H reconstruction
cost_impact: Seconds to minutes.
agent_guidance: For 'does it stay folded', DSSP is the claim-grade evidence and RMSD is the screening signal. Report the total helix+sheet fraction versus time: a stable plateau within a few percent of the starting value = folded. Loss concentrated in one terminal helix = local fraying, not unfolding — state that distinction. Requires an all-atom trajectory of the protein, so do not set compressed-x-grps to C-alpha-only if DSSP is requested.

label: Principal component analysis (essential dynamics)
meaning: Diagonalizes the covariance matrix of atomic fluctuations to extract the few collective modes that carry most of the motion, and projects the trajectory onto them to reveal the conformational landscape.
unit: eigenvalues in nm^2; projections in nm
default: {group: 'C-alpha', fit_group: 'Backbone', n_eigenvectors: 10, projections: [1,2], cosine_content: true, begin_ps: analysis.begin_ps}
options: outputs: eigenvalue spectrum | 2D projection (PC1 vs PC2) | extreme-structure PDB | cosine content
depends_on: stage.duration_ps (needs far more sampling than RMSD)
cost_impact: covar builds a 3N x 3N matrix: use C-alpha only (N ~ hundreds) — full-atom covar on a large protein is memory-heavy and slow.
agent_guidance: Compute PCA on C-alpha atoms after removing global rotation/translation, on the equilibrated window only. ALWAYS report the cosine content of PC1: a value near 1 means the 'collective mode' is indistinguishable from random diffusion, i.e. the trajectory is too short and the PCA is an artifact — in that case extend the run instead of interpreting the modes. This is the standard trap and the agent must check it.

label: Alchemical free energy protocol
meaning: A lambda-staged protocol that gradually decouples a molecule's interactions with its environment across a ladder of lambda windows, from which a free-energy difference (solvation or binding) is estimated by BAR/MBAR.
default: {couple_moltype: 'LIG', windows: 20, vdw_lambdas: 11, coul_lambdas: 5, sc_alpha: 0.5, sc_power: 1, sc_sigma: 0.3, nstdhdl: 10, estimator: 'BAR', equil_ps: 500, sample_ps: 5000}
depends_on: one MD job PER lambda window (this multiplies the GPU cost by the window count)
separate topology with a couplable moltype
cost_impact: MASSIVE: cost = n_windows x (equil + sample). 20 windows x 5 ns = 100 ns of MD for one number. This is the most expensive thing in the lab.
agent_guidance: Treat as a fan-out of independent jobs, one per lambda state, sharing everything but init-lambda-state — this maps naturally onto the scheduler queue and is the only way to fit it in a GPU budget. Rules that must not be broken: (1) turn OFF charges before vdW (decouple coulomb first, then vdW) or you get catastrophic overlaps; (2) soft-core (sc-alpha=0.5, sc-power=1, sc-sigma=0.3) is MANDATORY for the vdW ladder or lambda~1 windows will explode; (3) set nstdhdl=10 and calc-lambda-neighbors=-1 if you intend to use MBAR; (4) check the phase-space overlap matrix from gmx bar — poor overlap between adjacent windows means you must ADD windows, not run longer. Before proposing this, tell the user the total GPU cost up front; it is easy to burn a week of GPU here.

label: Energy terms to extract
meaning: Which .edr fields are pulled into the manifest time series — the thermodynamic evidence that the run was physically sound.
cost_impact: Seconds.
agent_guidance: Always extract Conserved-En. and Constr.-rmsd in addition to the obvious terms — they are the two cheapest correctness gates in all of MD. A drifting Conserved-En. means the timestep/cutoff/thermostat combination is unsound (shrink dt or fix the cutoffs); a Constr.-rmsd above ~1e-5 means LINCS is failing and the run is untrustworthy no matter how pretty the RMSD plot looks. Check Temperature mean == ref-t and Pressure mean == ref-p (pressure is very noisy; judge only the mean over >=1 ns, never an instantaneous value).

label: Equilibration gate (derived observable)
meaning: A boolean decision, computed from the .edr, that the system has reached a steady state and production is worth starting or analyzing: density/volume plateaued, temperature at the setpoint, potential energy no longer trending, and (for proteins) RMSD flattened.
options: checks: density_drift | temperature_mean | potential_energy_drift | rmsd_plateau | constr_rmsd | conserved_energy_drift
cost_impact: none
agent_guidance: The agent should evaluate this gate BETWEEN stages, not only at the end. If NPT has not plateaued, lengthen NPT rather than starting production. If production's first half and second half disagree on the observable of interest, extend rather than report. This gate plus stage.extend_ps is the entire self-correction loop.

label: Record resolved .mdp and versions in the manifest
meaning: Store the fully resolved per-stage .mdp text, seeds, force field, GROMACS version, and command lines in run.json so a result can be reproduced, audited, and diffed against a failed run.
cost_impact: A few kB per run.
agent_guidance: Non-negotiable for an agent-driven lab: without the resolved .mdp the agent cannot answer 'what was different about the run that worked', cannot reproduce a result, and cannot diff a crashed run against a healthy one. Also record the mdrun performance line (ns/day) — it is the empirical basis for every future cost estimate.
